en.extract:
The pin-tailed green pigeon or pin-tailed pigeon (Treron apicauda) is a species of bird in the family Columbidae native to Southeast Asia. 


Description

The pin-tailed green pigeon is a medium sized dove, with an average weight of 185-255 grams. Males are 32-36 cm and females are typically 28 cm. It is yellow-green overall, with darker green wing-coverts and scapulars and black outer secondaries and primaries. The males have a golden breast with pinkish hues. The central tail feathers are a bluish-gray and the undertail-coverts a chestnut color. The orbital ring is blue, as is the bill, with a lighter green or yellow tip. The legs and feet are a bright reddish-pink. The females are a duller yellow, with much shorter central tail feathers and dull undertail-coverts.
The song is described as a soft whistling ku-koo, which is usually paired. The pin-tailed green pigeon also lets out a high pitched doo! call. While in flight, feathers produce a whirring sound.


Taxonomy and systematics
The pin-tailed green pigeon was first described by Edward Blyth in 1846. The species' generic name comes from the Greek trērōn (timid, shy dove), while the specific epithet apicauda is derived from a combination of the Latin apicis (apex or point) and the Latin cauda (tail). The pin-tailed green pigeon was placed in the genus Sphenerus in the past.


Subspecies
There are three recognized subspecies of the pin-tailed green pigeon.

Treron apicauda apicauda.  It is found in Bangladesh, Bhutan, China, Myanmar, Nepal and Thailand.
Treron apicauda laotianus.  Previously misspelt laotinus. It is found in the mountains of Laos and Vietnam.
Treron apicauda lowei. It is found in the mountains of Thailand, central Laos, and Vietnam.


Distribution and habitat
The pin-tailed green pigeon is found in Bangladesh, Bhutan, Cambodia, China, India, Laos, Myanmar, Nepal, Thailand, and Vietnam. It inhabits secondary growth and forest, including foothill forests, subtropical and tropical dry forest, and evergreen rainforest. It typically inhabits areas 0 to 1800 meters in elevation.
Pin-tailed green pigeons are resident birds in some parts of their range, and nomadic in other parts in response to food resources. In central Vietnam, they will descend to lower elevations during the dry season, which is when many trees are fruiting. 


Behavior and ecology


Diet
The pin-tailed green pigeon is frugivorous, feeding mainly on fruits and berries in an acrobatic fashion. The species is gregarious, forming feeding flocks varying from 10-30 birds. Flocks have been observed descending to the ground to visit salt licks. 


Breeding
Nesting has been observed in April, May, and June in the Himalayan foothills. In other parts of Southeast Asia, pin-tailed green pigeons have been observed breeding year round. The female lays two white eggs in a platformed twig nest, usually 5-6 meters off the ground. Nests have been observed in bamboo thickets, trees, and shrubs. Both parents incubate and feed the young. Individuals have a generational length of approximately 4.2 years.


Conservation status
The pin-tailed green pigeon was assessed by the IUCN in 2018 and was listed as a species of least concern, though the population trend is decreasing. Species is thought to be common across much of its range, though sightings are limited. Scarce in Thailand, and reported as very rare in Bangladesh.


References

The '''pin-tailed green pigeon''' or '''pin-tailed pigeon''' ('''''Treron apicauda''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Columbidae]] native to [[Southeast Asia]]. 

== Description ==
[[File:4G4A9389.jpg|thumb|Pin-tailed green pigeon]]
The pin-tailed green pigeon is a medium sized dove, with an average weight of 185-255 grams. Males are 32-36 cm and females are typically 28 cm. It is yellow-green overall, with darker green wing-coverts and scapulars and black outer secondaries and primaries. The males have a golden breast with pinkish hues. The central tail feathers are a bluish-gray and the [[Undertail coverts|undertail-coverts]] a chestnut color. The orbital ring is blue, as is the bill, with a lighter green or yellow tip. The legs and feet are a bright reddish-pink. The females are a duller yellow, with much shorter central tail feathers and dull undertail-coverts.<ref name=":0">{{Citation |last1=Baptista |first1=Luis F. |title=Pin-tailed Green-Pigeon (Treron apicauda) |date=2020-03-04 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/pitpig1/1.0/introduction |work=Birds of the World |editor-last=Billerman |editor-first=Shawn M. |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.pitpig1.01 |access-date=2022-02-27 |last2=Trail |first2=Pepper W. |last3=Horblit |first3=H.M. |last4=Boesman |first4=Peter F. D. |last5=Garcia |first5=Ernest |s2cid=241088034 |editor2-last=Keeney |editor2-first=Brooke K. |editor3-last=Rodewald |editor3-first=Paul G. |editor4-last=Schulenberg |editor4-first=Thomas S.|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The song is described as a soft whistling ''ku-koo'', which is usually paired. The pin-tailed green pigeon also lets out a high pitched ''doo!'' call. While in flight, feathers produce a whirring sound.<ref name=":0" />

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The pin-tailed green pigeon was first described by [[Edward Blyth]] in 1846.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Blyth |date=1846 |title=Treron apicauda apicauda |url=https://www.gbif.org/species/6179553 |access-date=2022-02-27 |website=www.gbif.org |language=en}}</ref> The species' generic name comes from the [[Greek language|Greek]] ''trērōn'' (timid, shy dove),<ref>{{Cite web |title=Definition of TRERON |url=https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Treron |access-date=2022-02-27 |website=www.merriam-webster.com |language=en}}</ref> while the specific epithet ''apicauda'' is derived from a combination of the [[Latin]] ''apicis'' (apex or point){{cn|date=March 2022}} and the Latin ''cauda'' (tail).<ref>{{Cite web |title=Medical Definition of CAUDA |url=https://www.merriam-webster.com/medical/cauda |access-date=2022-02-27 |website=www.merriam-webster.com |language=en}}</ref> The pin-tailed green pigeon was placed in the genus ''Sphenerus'' in the past.<ref name=":0" />

=== Subspecies ===
There are three recognized subspecies of the pin-tailed green pigeon.

* ''Treron apicauda apicauda.'' <ref name=":1" /> It is found in [[Bangladesh]], [[Bhutan]], [[China]], [[Myanmar]], [[Nepal]] and [[Thailand]].<ref name=":0" />
* ''Treron apicauda laotianus.'' <ref>{{Cite web |last=Delacour |date=1926 |title=Treron apicauda laotinus (Pin-tailed Green-Pigeon (laotinus)) - Avibase |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=4AF8D9325436B76C |access-date=2022-02-27 |website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref> Previously misspelt ''laotinus''. It is found in the mountains of [[Laos]] and [[Vietnam]].<ref name=":0" />
* ''Treron apicauda lowei''.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Delacour |date=1924 |title=Treron apicauda lowei (Pin-tailed Green-Pigeon (lowei)) - Avibase |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=22CBE93CA6349AB6 |access-date=2022-02-27 |website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref> It is found in the mountains of [[Thailand]], central [[Laos]], and [[Vietnam]].<ref name=":0" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
The pin-tailed green pigeon is found in [[Bangladesh]], [[Bhutan]], [[Cambodia]], [[China]], [[India]], [[Laos]], [[Myanmar]], [[Nepal]], [[Thailand]], and [[Vietnam]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Husain |first=K. Z. |title=Subdivisions and Zoogeography of the Genus Treron (Green Fruit-Pigeons) |date=July 1958 |journal=Ibis |volume=100 |issue=3 |pages=334–348 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1958.tb00403.x |via=Wiley}}</ref> It inhabits secondary growth and forest, including foothill forests, subtropical and tropical dry forest, and evergreen rainforest. It typically inhabits areas 0 to 1800 meters in elevation.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |date=August 7, 2018 |title=Pin-tailed Green-pigeon |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22691252/130177627#population |access-date=February 27, 2022 |website=IUCN Red List}}</ref>

Pin-tailed green pigeons are resident birds in some parts of their range, and nomadic in other parts in response to food resources.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hubbard |first1=John P. |last2=Seymour |first2=Charles |title=Some Notable Bird Records from Egypt |date=2008-04-03 |journal=Ibis |volume=110 |issue=4 |pages=575–578 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1968.tb00067.x |issn=0019-1019}}</ref> In central Vietnam, they will descend to lower elevations during the dry season, which is when many trees are fruiting. 

== Behavior and ecology ==

=== Diet ===
The pin-tailed green pigeon is [[Frugivore|frugivorous]], feeding mainly on fruits and berries in an acrobatic fashion.<ref name=":0" /> The species is [[Sociality|gregarious]], forming feeding flocks varying from 10-30 birds.<ref name=":2" /> Flocks have been observed descending to the ground to visit salt licks. 

=== Breeding ===
Nesting has been observed in April, May, and June in the [[Himalayan foothills]]. In other parts of Southeast Asia, pin-tailed green pigeons have been observed breeding year round. The female lays two white eggs in a platformed twig nest, usually 5-6 meters off the ground. Nests have been observed in bamboo thickets, trees, and shrubs. Both parents incubate and feed the young. Individuals have a generational length of approximately 4.2 years.<ref name=":2" />

== Conservation status ==
The pin-tailed green pigeon was assessed by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] in 2018 and was listed as a species of [[Least-concern species|least concern]], though the population trend is decreasing.<ref name=":2" /> Species is thought to be common across much of its range, though sightings are limited. Scarce in Thailand, and reported as very rare in Bangladesh.<ref name=":0" />

[[File:TreronApicauda.jpg|thumb|left|Artist's illustration]]

zh.extract:
针尾绿鸠（学名：Treron apicauda）为鸠鸽科绿鸠属的鸟类。分布于喜马拉雅山脉以南、以至印度、中南半岛至马来半岛以及中国大陆的四川、云南等地，一般生活于山区常绿阔叶林中。该物种的模式产地在喜马拉雅山脉东南地区。


保护
中国国家重点保护动物等级：二级


亚种
针尾绿鸠指名亚种（学名：Treron apicauda apicauda）。分布于自印度至缅甸以及中国大陆的四川、云南等地。该物种的模式产地在喜马拉雅东南部。
针尾绿鸠滇南亚种（学名：Treron apicauda laotinus）。分布于中南半岛以及中国大陆的云南等地。该物种的模式产地在老挝。
